name: Integrals
subject: Maths

sections.heading: 7.1 Integration as Anti-Differentiation
sections.body:
An antiderivative (or primitive) of $f$ is a function $F$ with $F'(x)=f(x)$. The collection of all antiderivatives is the indefinite integral:

$\int f(x)\,dx=F(x)+C,$ where $C$ is the constant of integration.

Linearity: $\int[af(x)+bg(x)]dx=a\!\int f(x)dx+b\!\int g(x)dx.$

sections.heading: 7.2 Standard Integrals
sections.body:
• $\int x^{n}dx=\dfrac{x^{n+1}}{n+1}+C,\;n\neq-1$.
  • $\int\dfrac{dx}{x}=\ln|x|+C$.
  • $\int e^{x}dx=e^{x}+C$, $\int a^{x}dx=\dfrac{a^{x}}{\ln a}+C$.
  • $\int\sin x\,dx=-\cos x+C$, $\int\cos x\,dx=\sin x+C$.
  • $\int\sec^{2}x\,dx=\tan x+C$, $\int\csc^{2}x\,dx=-\cot x+C$.
  • $\int\sec x\tan x\,dx=\sec x+C$, $\int\csc x\cot x\,dx=-\csc x+C$.
  • $\int\dfrac{dx}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}=\sin^{-1}x+C$, $\int\dfrac{dx}{1+x^{2}}=\tan^{-1}x+C$, $\int\dfrac{dx}{x\sqrt{x^{2}-1}}=\sec^{-1}|x|+C$.

sections.heading: 7.3 Integration by Substitution
sections.body:
If $u=g(x)$, $du=g'(x)dx$, then $\int f(g(x))\,g'(x)\,dx=\int f(u)\,du.$

Useful patterns:

  • $\int\tan x\,dx=\ln|\sec x|+C$ (substitute $u=\cos x$).
  • $\int\sec x\,dx=\ln|\sec x+\tan x|+C$.
  • $\int\csc x\,dx=\ln|\csc x-\cot x|+C$.
  • $\int\dfrac{f'(x)}{f(x)}dx=\ln|f(x)|+C$.

sections.heading: 7.4 Integration by Partial Fractions
sections.body:
For a proper rational function $\dfrac{P(x)}{Q(x)}$ with $\deg P<\deg Q$, decompose $\dfrac{P}{Q}$ into a sum of simpler fractions corresponding to the linear and irreducible quadratic factors of $Q$.

Standard forms:

  • Each linear factor $(ax+b)\to\dfrac{A}{ax+b}$.
  • Repeated linear $(ax+b)^{k}\to\dfrac{A_1}{ax+b}+\dfrac{A_2}{(ax+b)^{2}}+\dots+\dfrac{A_k}{(ax+b)^{k}}$.
  • Quadratic $(ax^{2}+bx+c)$ irreducible$\to\dfrac{Ax+B}{ax^{2}+bx+c}$.

If improper, perform polynomial division first.

sections.heading: 7.5 Integration by Parts
sections.body:
$\int u\,dv=uv-\int v\,du.$ Equivalently, $\int u(x)\,v'(x)\,dx=u(x)v(x)-\int v(x)\,u'(x)\,dx.$

ILATE/LIATE rule for choosing $u$: Inverse trig, Logarithmic, Algebraic, Trigonometric, Exponential — pick $u$ as the one earlier in the list.

Useful identity. $\int e^{x}[f(x)+f'(x)]\,dx=e^{x}f(x)+C.$

sections.heading: 7.7 Definite Integral & Fundamental Theorem of Calculus
sections.body:
The definite integral $\int_{a}^{b} f(x)\,dx$ represents the signed area under $y=f(x)$ between $x=a$ and $x=b$. Defined as a limit of Riemann sums.

FTC Part 1. If $A(x)=\int_{a}^{x}f(t)\,dt$ then $A'(x)=f(x)$.

FTC Part 2. If $F$ is any antiderivative of $f$ then $\int_{a}^{b}f(x)\,dx=F(b)-F(a).$

sections.heading: 7.8 Properties of Definite Integrals
sections.body:
1. $\int_a^b f(x)\,dx=\int_a^b f(t)\,dt$ (rename dummy variable).
  2. $\int_a^b f\,dx=-\int_b^a f\,dx$.
  3. $\int_a^b f\,dx=\int_a^c f\,dx+\int_c^b f\,dx$.
  4. $\int_a^b f(x)\,dx=\int_a^b f(a+b-x)\,dx$.
  5. $\int_0^a f(x)\,dx=\int_0^a f(a-x)\,dx$.
  6. $\int_0^{2a}f(x)\,dx=\int_0^a [f(x)+f(2a-x)]\,dx$; equals $2\int_0^a f$ if $f(2a-x)=f(x)$, equals $0$ if $f(2a-x)=-f(x)$.
  7. $\int_{-a}^{a}f(x)\,dx=2\int_0^{a}f(x)\,dx$ if $f$ even; $=0$ if $f$ odd.

summary: Indefinite integration inverts differentiation up to the constant $C$. The standard table covers powers, exponentials, trig, and inverse-trig forms. Substitution converts $\int f(g(x))g'(x)dx$ to $\int f(u)du$; partial fractions splits rational integrands; integration by parts uses $\int u\,dv=uv-\int v\,du$ with ILATE choice. Special-form integrals handle $1/(x^{2}\pm a^{2})$, $1/\sqrt{a^{2}-x^{2}}$, $\sqrt{x^{2}\pm a^{2}}$. Definite integrals compute net signed areas via FTC: $\int_a^b f=F(b)-F(a)$, with symmetry properties simplifying many evaluations.
pitfalls: Wrong sign on $\int\sin x\,dx$ — it is $-\cos x+C$.
Doing partial fractions on an improper rational without dividing first.
Choosing $u$ wrong in integration by parts — apply ILATE.
Substituting in a definite integral but forgetting to change the limits.
Treating $\int_a^b f\,dx$ as positive area even when $f<0$ on part of $[a,b]$.
